fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a maps API wrapper library that handles building request URLs and parsing responses. The idea is developers shouldn't have to manually stitch together query strings or dig through nested JSON — the library takes structured inputs and spits out the right URLs or parsed summaries. We support a bunch of different service types: routing between two points, matrix distances, address lookups, place searches (nearby, text-based, details, photos, autocomplete variants), plus the timezone and geolocation ones which behave a bit differently from the rest (remember how we handled the alternate host situation in that geolocation spike last quarter — same idea here). For place photos specifically there's a size constraint thing we discussed — refer back to how we handled the 'at least one required field' pattern from the nearby search ranking validation. Coordinate pairs should always come out as lat,lng without any encoding. Address strings on the other hand do need proper percent-encoding. For the parsing side, when we get back raw JSON from any of these services we want a flat summary output — status, key counts, first meaningful fields — not a full object model. Also there's a shared time value thing for opening hours that needs to handle day indexing and reject malformed time strings with a neutral error. Calendar time format is exactly 4 digits or it should fail. Make sure the multi-origin/destination matrix endpoint joins entries correctly and that the transit mode list preserves order with pipe separators.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: the geolocation one really does live on its own host and should be treated differently from everything else. The URL there is https://www.googleapis.com/geolocation/v1/geolocate?key=<API_KEY>, not anything on maps.googleapis.com. By contrast, all the other endpoints we’re wrapping here — directions, distance_matrix, geocoding, places_*, timezone — stay under https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/ . Same separation as before, just calling it out more explicitly so nobody accidentally routes geolocation through the common base.

A couple extra specifics on URL building too. For timezone, the endpoint is https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/timezone/json and it requires location and timestamp, with location rendered as lat,lng and timestamp as integer seconds. Language is optional, and the parameter order should be location, timestamp, language (if present), key. On coordinate formatting generally, lat/lng objects should always come through as bare lat,lng strings like -33.8670522,151.1957362 with NO percent-encoding applied. Only free-text address strings get percent-encoded, so spaces as %20, commas as %2C, etc. For distance_matrix specifically, if there are multiple origins or destinations, join them with |, and make sure each address string is percent-encoded on its own before joining. Coordinate entries there still stay as lat,lng with no encoding.

Also tightening up the place photo behavior since that got asked twice. The path is /maps/api/place/photo with no .json extension, and the query names are not the same as the input names: photo_reference → photoreference, max_height → maxheight, max_width → maxwidth. And if neither max_height nor max_width is provided, the output must be exactly: error=invalid_request\nrule=photo_size_required\n. That’s the same basic pattern as the places_nearby rankby=distance validation we already talked about: if the required piece isn’t there, return error=invalid_request plus the named rule.

trigger_keywords: transit mode pipe join
transit_mode list order
multiple transit modes
transit mode separator
transit modes serialization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When multiple transit_mode values are provided, they must be joined with a pipe character | and appended as a single transit_mode query parameter, preserving the original list order exactly as supplied.

trigger_keywords: calendar time format
four digit time
opening hours time parse
time string validation
day index weekday numbering
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The calendar_time endpoint expects a 'time' string of exactly 4 digits (e.g., '2330'). Any other length produces: error=invalid_argument
field=time
expected=four_digit_time
. The day_index is 0-based input but the output weekday field is 1-based (so day_index=0 → weekday=1). Hour is the first two digits, minute is the last two.

trigger_keywords: places_details both identifiers
place_id and reference conflict
ambiguous place identifier
exactly one identifier rule
place details validation error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The places_details endpoint requires exactly one of place_id or reference. If both are supplied simultaneously, output must be: error=invalid_request\nrule=exactly_one_place_identifier\n. The query parameter for place_id is 'placeid' (no underscore).

trigger_keywords: nearby rankby distance no keyword
rankby distance validation
distance ranking without filter
rank_by distance error
places nearby invalid ranking
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When rank_by=distance is used in places_nearby, at least one of keyword, name, or type must also be provided. Combining rank_by=distance with radius is also invalid. Violations produce: error=invalid_request\nrule=<specific_rule_identifier>\n.

trigger_keywords: parse directions output fields
directions response summary
directions JSON parsing fields
first_step_mode format
response parsing flat output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Parsing a directions response must output these fields in order: status, is_okay (true/false), routes (count), geocoded_waypoints (count), summary (first route), leg_steps (count of steps in first leg), start_address, end_address, first_step_mode (lowercased travel_mode of first step). All values are flat key=value lines.

trigger_keywords: geocoding bounds format
bounds northeast southwest
bounds pipe separator
bounds query parameter
bounding box URL encoding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The bounds parameter for geocoding must render as northeast_lat,northeast_lng|southwest_lat,southwest_lng — two coordinate corners separated by a pipe character |, with no percent-encoding applied to the coordinates or the pipe.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: how we handled the 'at least one required field' pattern
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Validation errors are rendered as flat key=value lines: error=invalid_request\nrule=<rule_name>\n — not as JSON or exception stack traces. This is the normalized error contract across all endpoints.

context_pointers.vague_reference: calendar time format is exactly 4 digits or it should fail
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
The calendar_time shared value parser in feature13_shared_value_parsing.json requires the time string to be exactly 4 characters long. '2330' is valid; '230' (3 digits) is not and returns error=invalid_argument
field=time
expected=four_digit_time
.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. Please check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature — each file contains the exact expected input/output contract. If you need clarification on a business rule, try looking at the feature description in start.md or pinging the original API integration team.

feature10_places_query_autocomplete_urls.description: Build a query autocomplete URL from a partial search phrase with optional offset, location bias, radius, and language.

feature11_time_and_geolocation_urls.json.description: Build request URLs for time zone lookup and browser/mobile geolocation lookup, preserving the distinct service host and query parameters required by each endpoint.

feature12_response_parsing.json.description: Parse JSON response bodies from routing, distance, geocoding, place search, autocomplete, time zone, and geolocation services into observable status and field summaries.

routes=1
geocoded_waypoints=4

leg_steps=1

end_address=Los Angeles, CA, USA
first_step_mode=driving

status=OK
is_okay=true
origin_addresses=1
destination_addresses=1
rows=1
origin_first=Bakshi Bazar Road, Dhaka, Bangladesh
destination_first=Rd 11, Dhaka 1212, Bangladesh
first_duration_text=29 mins
first_distance_value=9247

feature13_shared_value_parsing.json.description: Convert shared JSON value objects for coordinates and opening-hours day/time values into normalized observable fields, rejecting malformed time strings with a neutral validation error.

feature1_directions_endpoints.json.description: Build a directions request URL from origin and destination inputs, accepting either textual addresses or latitude/longitude coordinates and rejecting unsupported endpoint values.

feature2_directions_options.json.description: Build a directions request URL with optional route constraints, timing, transit preferences, waypoint lists, units, and alternative-route flags serialized into query parameters.

feature3_distance_matrix_urls.json.description: Build a distance matrix request URL from lists of origins and destinations, with optional travel filters and timing serialized into query parameters.

feature4_geocoding_urls.json.description: Build a geocoding request URL for address lookup, coordinate lookup, place identifier lookup, bounds, language, region, and component filters.

feature5_places_nearby_urls.json.description: Build a nearby place search URL from a coordinate, either radius-based or distance-ranked, including search filters and normalized validation errors for invalid ranking combinations.

feature6_places_text_urls.json.description: Build a text place search URL from a text query with optional location bias, radius, language, price bounds, open-now flag, page token, and place category.

feature7_places_details_urls.json.description: Build a place details URL using exactly one place identifier source, optionally selecting fields or language, and return a normalized validation error for ambiguous identifiers.

feature8_places_photo_urls.json.description: Build a place photo URL from a photo reference and at least one requested maximum dimension, returning a normalized validation error when no dimension is provided.

feature9_places_autocomplete_urls.json.description: Build a place autocomplete URL from a partial input string with optional offset, spatial bias, language, type filters, component filters, and strict-bound preference.
